title: Skipper Iresonï؟½s Ride
content:
آ آ آ آ In the valuable and carefully prepared History of Marblehead, published in 1879 by Samuel Roads, Jr., it is stated that the crew of Captain Ireson, rather than himself, were responsible for the abandonment of the disabled vessel. To screen themselves they charged their captain with the crime. In view of this the writer of the ballad addressed the following letter to the historian:

آ آ آ آ OAK KNOLL, DANVERS, 5 mo. 18, 1880.
آ آ آ آ MY DEAR FRIEND: I heartily thank thee for a copy of thy History of Marblehead. I have read it with great interest and think good use has been made of the abundant material. No town in Essex County has a record more honorable than Marblehead; no one has done more to develop the industrial interests of our New England seaboard, and certainly none have given such evidence of self-sacrificing patriotism. I am glad the story of it has been at last told, and told so well. I have now no doubt that thy version of Skipper Iresonï؟½s ride is the correct one. My verse was founded solely on a fragment of rhyme which I heard from one of my early schoolmates, a native of Marblehead. I supposed the story to which it referred dated back at least a century. I knew nothing of the participators, and the narrative of the ballad was pure fancy. I am glad for the sake of truth and justice that the real facts are given in thy book. I certainly would not knowingly do injustice to any one, dead or living.

آ آ آ آ I am very truly thy friend,
آ آ آ آ - JOHN G. WHITTIER.

آ آ آ آ Of all the rides since, the birth of time,
آ آ آ آ Told in story or sung in rhyme,
آ آ آ آ On Apuleiusï؟½s Golden Ass,
آ آ آ آ Or one-eyed Calendarï؟½s horse of brass;
آ آ آ آ Witch astride of a human back,
آ آ آ آ Islamï؟½s prophet on Al-Borï؟½k,
آ آ آ آ The strangest ride that ever was sped
آ آ آ آ Was Iresonï؟½s, out from Marblehead!
آ آ آ آ Old Floyd Ireson, for his hard heart,
آ آ آ آ Tarred and feathered and carried in a cart
آ آ آ آ By the women of Marblehead!

آ آ آ آ Body of turkey, head of owl,
آ آ آ آ Wings a-droop like a rained-on fowl,
آ آ آ آ Feathered and ruffled in every part,
آ آ آ آ Skipper Ireson stood in the cart.
آ آ آ آ Scores of women, old and young,
آ آ آ آ Strong of muscle, and glib of tongue,
آ آ آ آ Pushed and pulled up the rocky lane,
آ آ آ آ Shouting and singing the shrill refrain
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hereï؟½s Flud Oirson, fur his horrd horrt,
آ آ آ آ Torrï؟½d anï؟½ futherrï؟½d anï؟½ corrï؟½d in a corrt
آ آ آ آ By the women oï؟½ Morbleï؟½ead!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Wrinkled scolds with hands on hips,
آ آ آ آ Girls in bloom of cheek and lips,
آ آ آ آ Wild-eyed, free-limbed, such as chase
آ آ آ آ Bacchus round some antique vase,
آ آ آ آ Brief of skirt, with ankles bare,
آ آ آ آ Loose of kerchief and loose of hair,
آ آ آ آ With conch-shells blowing and fish-hornsï؟½ twang,
آ آ آ آ Over and over the Mï؟½nads sang
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hereï؟½s Flud Oirson, fur his horrd horrt,
آ آ آ آ Torrï؟½d anï؟½ futherrï؟½d an dorrï؟½d in a corrt
آ آ آ آ By the women oï؟½ Morbleï؟½ead!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Small pity for him! He sailed away
آ آ آ آ From a leaking ship, in Chaleur Bay,
آ آ آ آ Sailed away from a sinking wreck,
آ آ آ آ With his own townï؟½s-people on her deck!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Lay by! lay by!ï؟½ they called to him.
آ آ آ آ Back he answered, ï؟½Sink or swim!
آ آ آ آ Brag of your catch of fish again!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And off he sailed through the fog and rain!
آ آ آ آ Old Floyd Ireson, for his hard heart,
آ آ آ آ Tarred and feathered and carried in a cart
آ آ آ آ By the women of Marblehead!

آ آ آ آ Fathoms deep in dark Chaleur
آ آ آ آ That wreck shall lie forevermore.
آ آ آ آ Mother and sister, wife and maid,
آ آ آ آ Looked from the rocks of Marblehead
آ آ آ آ Over the moaning and rainy sea,
آ آ آ آ Looked for the coming that might not be!
آ آ آ آ What did the winds and the sea-birds say
آ آ آ آ Of the cruel captain who sailed away?
آ آ آ آ Old Floyd Ireson, for his hard heart,
آ آ آ آ Tarred and feathered and carried in a cart
آ آ آ آ By the women of Marblehead!

آ آ آ آ Through the street, on either side,
آ آ آ آ Up flew windows, doors swung wide;
آ آ آ آ Sharp-tongued spinsters, old wives gray,
آ آ آ آ Treble lent the fish-hornï؟½s bray.
آ آ آ آ Sea-worn grandsires, cripple-bound,
آ آ آ آ Hulks of old sailors run aground,
آ آ آ آ Shook head, and fist, and hat, and cane,
آ آ آ آ And cracked with curses the hoarse refrain
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hereï؟½s Flud Oirson, fur his horrd horrt,
آ آ آ آ Torrï؟½d anï؟½ futherrï؟½d anï؟½ corrï؟½d in a corrt
آ آ آ آ By the women oï؟½ï؟½Morbleï؟½ead!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Sweetly along the Salem road
آ آ آ آ Bloom of orchard and lilac showed.
آ آ آ آ Little the wicked skipper knew
آ آ آ آ Of the fields so green and the sky so blue.
آ آ آ آ Riding there in his sorry trim,
آ آ آ آ Like to Indian idol glum and grim,
آ آ آ آ Scarcely he seemed the sound to hear
آ آ آ آ Of voices shouting, far and near
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hereï؟½s Flud Oirson, fur his horrd horrt,
آ آ آ آ Torrï؟½d anï؟½ futherrï؟½d anï؟½ corrï؟½d in a corrt
آ آ آ آ By the women oï؟½ Morbleï؟½ead!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hear me, neighbors!ï؟½ at last he cried,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½What to me is this noisy ride?
آ آ آ آ What is the shame that clothes the skin
آ آ آ آ To the nameless horror that lives within?
آ آ آ آ Waking or sleeping, I see a wreck,
آ آ آ آ And hear a cry from a reeling deck!
آ آ آ آ Hate me and curse me, I only dread
آ آ آ آ The hand of God and the face of the dead!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Said old Floyd Ireson, for his hard heart,
آ آ آ آ Tarred and feathered and carried in a cart
آ آ آ آ By the women of Marblehead!

آ آ آ آ Then the wife of the skipper lost at sea
آ آ آ آ Said, ï؟½God has touched him! why should we?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Said an old wife mourning her only son,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Cut the rogueï؟½s tether and let him run!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ So with soft relentings and rude excuse,
آ آ آ آ Half scorn, half pity, they cut him loose,
آ آ آ آ And gave him a cloak to hide him in,
آ آ آ آ And left him alone with his shame and sin.
آ آ آ آ Poor Floyd Ireson, for his hard heart,
آ آ آ آ Tarred and feathered and carried in a cart
آ آ آ آ By the women of Marblehead!
meaning:
This evocative piece by John Greenleaf Whittier represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of John Greenleaf Whittier's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
